title.zh: 卷三十上 蘇竟楊厚列傳
title.en: Volume 30a: Biographies of Su Jing; Yang Hou
title.raw: 卷三十上 蘇竟楊厚列傳 Volume 30a: Biographies of Su Jing; Yang Hou

sentences.zh: 蘇竟字伯況，扶風平陵人也。

translations.literal: Su Jing, courtesy name Bokuang, was a native of Pingling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jing, whose courtesy name was Bokuang, came from Pingling in Fufeng commandery.

sentences.zh: 平帝世，竟以明《易》為博士講《書》祭酒。

translations.literal: In the era of Emperor Ping, Jing, because he was proficient in the Changes, became Erudite Libationer for lectures on the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ping, Su Jing’s mastery of the Book of Changes earned him appointment as Erudite Libationer charged with expounding the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 善圖緯，能通百家之言。

translations.literal: He was skilled in charts and weft texts and could embrace the teachings of the hundred schools.
translations.idiomatic: He excelled at prognostic charts and apocryphal "weft" texts and commanded the literature of the hundred schools.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，與劉歆等共典校書，拜代郡中尉。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang’s time, together with Liu Xin and others he jointly supervised collation of the classics and was appointed Commandant of Dai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang he joined Liu Xin and others in overseeing editorial work on the palace books and received appointment as commandant of Dai commandery.

sentences.zh: 時匈奴擾亂，北邊多罹其禍，竟終完輯一郡。

translations.literal: At that time the Xiongnu caused turmoil, and the northern frontier mostly suffered their harm; Jing in the end preserved and pacified the entire commandery intact.
translations.idiomatic: While the Xiongnu ravaged the north and most border regions suffered, Su Jing nevertheless kept Dai commandery intact and at peace.

sentences.zh: 光武即位，就拜代郡太守，使固塞以拒匈奴。

translations.literal: When Guangwu ascended the throne, he was appointed on the spot as Administrator of Dai commandery and ordered to strengthen the passes to resist the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Guangwu took the throne, Su Jing was confirmed as administrator of Dai commandery and tasked with fortifying the frontier against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 建武五年冬，盧芳略得北邊諸郡，帝使偏將軍隨弟屯代郡。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Jianwu, winter, Lu Fang seized control of the northern-border commanderies; the emperor sent Adjutant-General Sui Di to encamp in Dai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: In winter of Jianwu 5, Lu Fang overran the northern commanderies, and the emperor dispatched Adjutant-General Sui Di to station troops in Dai.

sentences.zh: 竟病篤，以兵屬弟，詣京師謝罪。

translations.literal: Jing was gravely ill; he handed the troops to his younger brother and went to the capital to apologize for his faults.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jing fell seriously ill, transferred military authority to his brother, and traveled to the capital to submit himself for punishment.

sentences.zh: 拜侍中，數月，以病免。

translations.literal: He was appointed Palace Attendant; after several months he was dismissed because of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He was made a palace attendant but within a few months resigned on grounds of ill health.

sentences.zh: 初，延岑護軍鄧仲況擁兵據南陽陰縣為寇，而劉歆兄子龔為其謀主。

translations.literal: Earlier, Deng Zhongkuang, protector-general under Yan Cen, had held troops and occupied Yinxian in Nanyang as a bandit, and Liu Gong, nephew of Liu Xin, was his chief strategist.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Deng Zhongkuang—protector-general to the warlord Yan Cen—had seized Yinxian in Nanyang as a rebel stronghold, with Liu Xin’s nephew Liu Gong as his principal adviser.

sentences.zh: 竟時在南陽，與龔書曉之曰：

translations.literal: Jing was at that time in Nanyang; he wrote Gong a letter to enlighten him, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Su Jing was then in Nanyang and sent Liu Gong a letter meant to bring him to reason.

sentences.zh: 君執事無恙。

translations.literal: May those who attend you be without illness.
translations.idiomatic: I trust those who serve you are all well.

sentences.zh: 走昔以摩研編削之才，與國師公從事出入，校定秘書，竊自依依，末由自遠。

translations.literal: I who formerly with the talent for polishing and editing went in and out in employment with the National Instructor, collating the secret books—secretly I clung to that tie myself, yet had no way to distance myself.
translations.idiomatic: In former days I went in and out at the National Instructor’s side with nothing more than editing skill, helping collate the palace library; I cherished that connection in private yet could never break free of it.

sentences.zh: 蓋聞君子湣同類而傷不遇。

translations.literal: I have heard that the gentleman pities those of his kind and grieves when they meet no proper recognition.
translations.idiomatic: They say a gentleman feels for his fellows and sorrows when worthy men go unrecognized.

sentences.zh: 人無愚智，莫不先避害然後求利，先定誌然後求名。

translations.literal: Among people there is none, foolish or wise, who does not first flee harm and then seek profit, first settle his purpose and then seek a name.
translations.idiomatic: Whether clever or dull, everyone first avoids danger, then pursues gain; first fixes intent, then pursues reputation.

sentences.zh: 昔智果見智伯窮兵必亡，故變名遠逝，陳平知項王為天所棄，故歸心高祖，皆智之至也。

translations.literal: Formerly Zhiguo saw that Zhibo’s endless warfare would bring ruin, and so changed his name and fled far away; Chen Ping knew that the Hegemon-King of Chu was abandoned by Heaven, and so turned his heart to Gaozu—these were the utmost in wisdom.
translations.idiomatic: Zhiguo saw Zhibo’s warmongering would destroy him and fled under an altered name; Chen Ping knew Xiang Yu was heaven-forsaken and gave his loyalty to Gaozu—both reached the height of discernment.

sentences.zh: 聞郡前權時屈節，北面延牙，乃後覺悟，棲遲養德。

translations.literal: I hear that your commandery head once temporized and bowed north to Yan Ya’s teeth, then afterward came to awareness and dwelt in retirement cultivating virtue.
translations.idiomatic: I hear your prefect once bent the knee to Yan Cen, then saw the light and withdrew to nurture his moral standing.

sentences.zh: 先世數子，又何以加。

translations.literal: The several sons of former generations—how could anything surpass them?
translations.idiomatic: How could the worthy examples of earlier generations be improved upon?

sentences.zh: 君處陰中，土多賢士，若以須臾之間，研考異同，揆之圖書，測之人事，則得失利害，可陳於目，何自負畔亂之困，不移守惡之名乎？

translations.literal: You dwell in obscurity, and the land has many worthy scholars; if in a brief span you examine and compare divergent views, measure them against the charts and documents and test them against human affairs, then gain and loss, benefit and harm can be arrayed before your eyes—why burden yourself with the straits of rebellion and cling to the reputation of clinging to evil?
translations.idiomatic: You live among talented men in obscurity; given a little time to weigh competing opinions against the charts and annals and the facts on the ground, you could see clearly what profits and what harms—why stay trapped in revolt and bear the stain of willful wrongdoing?

sentences.zh: 與君子之道，何其反也？

translations.literal: Compared with the way of the gentleman, how contrary is this?
translations.idiomatic: How does that square with the conduct of a gentleman?

sentences.zh: 世之俗儒末學，醒醉不分，而稽論當世，疑誤視聽。

translations.literal: The vulgar Ru scholars and petty learners of the age, not distinguishing wakefulness from drunkenness, yet pronounce judgment on the present age and mislead what is seen and heard.
translations.idiomatic: Common pedants of the day, too muddled to tell sense from nonsense, still presume to lecture on current affairs and confuse everyone who listens.

sentences.zh: 或謂天下叠興，未知誰是，稱兵據土，可圖非冀。

translations.literal: Some say the realm rises and falls in turns and no one knows who will prevail, so one may raise arms and hold territory and scheme for what is not to be hoped.
translations.idiomatic: Some claim dynasties rise in cycles and the winner is unknown, so seizing ground by force might win the unbelievable prize.

sentences.zh: 或曰聖王未啟，宜觀時變，倚強附大，顧望自守。

translations.literal: Some say the sage king has not yet appeared and one ought to watch how the times shift, lean on the strong and attach to the great, and look about while holding one’s own.
translations.idiomatic: Others say no true king has yet emerged, so one should read the times, side with the powerful, and wait while keeping one’s position.

sentences.zh: 二者之論，豈其然乎？

translations.literal: As for the theories of these two, could they really be so?
translations.idiomatic: Can either of those views actually hold water?

sentences.zh: 夫孔丘秘經，為漢赤制，玄包幽室，文隱事明。

translations.literal: As for Confucius’s secret classics, they establish the red mandate for Han; the dark wraps the hidden chamber—the text is concealed yet the matter is clear.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius’s cryptic classics spell out Han’s crimson mandate: mystery veils the inner chamber, the wording is obscure but the message is plain.

sentences.zh: 且火德承堯，雖昧必亮，承積世之祚，握無窮之符，王氏雖乘間偷篡，而終嬰大戮，支分體解，宗氏屠滅，非其效歟？

translations.literal: Moreover the Fire Virtue succeeds Yao; though dim it must shine bright; it inherits the fortune built across generations and grasps portents without end. Though the Wang clan seized a gap to steal the throne, in the end they suffered great execution—limbs torn apart, bodies split, the clan slaughtered—is this not its proof?
translations.idiomatic: Han’s fire virtue follows Yao’s line; even in gloom it must flare; it carries forward ages of fortune and holds inexhaustible tokens. The Wangs may have usurped in a moment of weakness, yet they ended in hideous execution—dismemberment and clan extinction—is that not demonstration enough?

sentences.zh: 皇天所以眷顧蜘躕，憂漢子孫者也。

translations.literal: This is why august Heaven turns its gaze and lingers: it cares for the descendants of Han.
translations.idiomatic: That is why High Heaven looks down with lingering concern: it is watching over Han’s heirs.

sentences.zh: 論者若不本之於天，參之於聖，猥以《師曠雜事》輕自眩惑，說士作書，亂夫大道，焉可信哉？

translations.literal: If debaters do not root their claims in Heaven and check them against the sages, but basely take Master Kuang’s miscellaneous tales to dazzle themselves at random, and wandering scholars compose books that disorder the great Way—how can such things be trusted?
translations.idiomatic: If disputants ignore Heaven and the sages and cheaply let odd tales like Master Kuang’s miscellany dizzy them, while scribblers churn out doctrine that derails the true path—why credit any of it?

sentences.zh: 諸儒或曰：今五星失晷，天時謬錯，辰星久而不效，太白出入過度，熒惑進退見態，鎮星繞帶天街，歲星不舍氐、房。

translations.literal: The various Ru may say: Now the five planets miss their gnomon shadows; the seasons of Heaven are in error; the chronograph star has long failed to appear as predicted; the Great White exceeds its proper comings and goings; the Sparkling Deluder advances and retreats showing phases; the Queller star loops girding the Celestial Street; the Year Star does not quit the lodges Di and Fang.
translations.idiomatic: Some classicists argue: the five planets stray from their predicted positions; the calendar is awry; Mercury has long failed to behave as expected; Venus crosses lines; Mars advances and retreats suspiciously; Saturn encircles the Celestial Street; Jupiter will not leave Di and Fang.

sentences.zh: 以為諸如此占，歸之國家。

translations.literal: They consider prognostications such as these to concern the state.
translations.idiomatic: They take such omens to bear on the dynasty itself.

sentences.zh: 蓋災不徒設，皆應之分野，各有所主。

translations.literal: Disasters are not set up for nothing; all correspond to field-allotments, each with its master.
translations.idiomatic: Calamities do not occur without meaning; each aligns with an astrological jurisdiction and its ruling house.

sentences.zh: 夫房、心即宋之分，東海是也。

translations.literal: Now Fang and Xin are Song’s allotment; that is Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Fang and Xin mark Song’s celestial segment—corresponding to Donghai.

sentences.zh: 尾為燕分，漁陽是也。

translations.literal: The Tail is Yan’s allotment; that is Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: The Tail constellation marks Yan’s segment—here, Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 東海董憲迷惑未降，漁陽彭寵逆亂擁兵，王赫斯怒，命將並征，故熒惑應此，憲、寵受殃。

translations.literal: In Donghai Dong Xian has been deluded and has not submitted; in Yuyang Peng Chong rebels with troops in hand. Our king blazes with wrath and has ordered generals to campaign together; therefore the Sparkling Deluder responds to this, and Xian and Chong meet disaster.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian still deludes himself in Donghai; Peng Chong holds Yuyang in revolt. The sovereign’s anger runs high and sends armies against them—so Mars answers accordingly, and both rebels reap punishment.

sentences.zh: 太白、辰星自亡新之末，失行算度，以至於今，或守東井，或沒羽林，或裴回藩屏，或躑躅帝宮，或經天反明，或潛臧久沈，或衰微暗昧，或煌煌北南，或盈縮成鉤，或偃蹇不禁，皆大運蕩除之祥，聖帝應符之兆也。

translations.literal: From the end of the lost Xin dynasty Great White and the chronograph star failed their reckoned courses down to the present—sometimes guarding the Eastern Well, sometimes sinking into the Feathered Forest, sometimes roaming the screens of the vassals, sometimes pacing the imperial palace, sometimes crossing the sky returning brightness, sometimes hiding long submerged, sometimes faint and dim, sometimes blazing north and south, sometimes waxing and waning into hooks, sometimes stumbling unrestrained—all are omens of the great destiny sweeping clean and signs that the sage emperor matches the talisman.
translations.idiomatic: Since Xin fell, Venus and Mercury have wandered off course—now guarding Dongjing, now hiding in the Feathered Forest, now lingering by feudal screens, now pacing the imperial stars, now shining across the zenith, now sunk from sight, now feeble, now brilliant to north and south, now swelling and shrinking into hooks, now staggering unchecked—all foretell the purge of fate and the sage emperor’s receipt of the mandate.

sentences.zh: 賊臣亂子，往往錯互，指麾妄說，傳相壞誤。

translations.literal: Rebellious ministers and disorderly sons constantly crisscross; they point and gesture with reckless explanations and pass corruption one to another.
translations.idiomatic: Traitors and rebel heirs contradict one another; charlatans gesture at the sky and spread garbled doctrine.

sentences.zh: 由此論之，天文安得遵度哉！

translations.literal: Judged by this, how can celestial patterns ever stay within measure?
translations.idiomatic: Given all this, how could astronomy ever stay neatly "on schedule"?

sentences.zh: 乃者，五月甲申，天有白虹，自子加午，廣可十丈，長可萬丈，正臨倚彌。

translations.literal: Recently, on jiashen in the fifth month, Heaven had a white rainbow from zi striking wu, about ten Chinese feet wide and ten thousand feet long, directly overlying Yimi.
translations.idiomatic: Lately, on a fifth-month jiashen day, a white arc spanned from zi to wu—some ten feet wide and immense in length—hovering directly above Yimi.

sentences.zh: 倚彌即黎丘，秦豐之都也。

translations.literal: Yimi is Liqiu, the capital of Qin Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Yimi is Liqiu, the seat of Qin Feng.

sentences.zh: 是時月入於畢。

translations.literal: At that time the moon entered Bi.
translations.idiomatic: That same time the moon entered the Bi lodge.

sentences.zh: 畢為天網，主網羅無道之君，故武王將伐紂，上祭於畢，求助天也。

translations.literal: Bi is Heaven’s net; it presides over netting lawless lords. Therefore when King Wu was about to attack Zhou, he sacrificed aloft at Bi, seeking aid from Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Bi is the celestial net that snares wicked rulers. King Wu therefore sacrificed at Bi before marching against Shang, invoking Heaven’s aid.

sentences.zh: 夫仲夏甲申為八魁。

translations.literal: Now midsummer jiashen is one of the Eight Chiefs (bagui).
translations.idiomatic: A jiashen day in midsummer falls among the Eight Chiefs (bagui, spirits of closure).

sentences.zh: 八魁，上帝開塞之將也，主退惡攘逆。

translations.literal: The Eight Chiefs are Heaven’s generals who open and block; they preside over driving off evil and rebelling against insurrection.
translations.idiomatic: Those Eight Chiefs are Heaven’s marshals of closure and release; they govern expulsion of wickedness and suppression of rebellion.

sentences.zh: 流星狀似蚩尤旗，或曰營頭，或曰天槍，出奎而西北行，至延牙營上，散為數百而滅，奎為毒螫，主庫兵。

translations.literal: A shooting star shaped like the Banner of Chiyou—some call it "camp head," some "sky lance"—issued from Kui and ran northwest to halt above Yan Ya’s camp, scattered into hundreds and vanished; Kui is venomous sting and presides over stored weapons.
translations.idiomatic: A meteor like Chiyou’s banner—called "camp head" or "sky lance"—rose from Kui, streaked northwest to Yan Cen’s camp, broke into hundreds of sparks, and died; Kui stands for poison sting and armory stores.

sentences.zh: 此二變，郡中及延牙士眾所共見也。

translations.literal: These two changes were jointly witnessed by the commandery and Yan Ya’s soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone in the district and Yan Cen’s troops saw both phenomena.

sentences.zh: 是故延牙遂之武當，托言發兵，實避其殃。

translations.literal: Therefore Yan Ya then went to Wudang, claiming he was raising troops but in truth avoiding that calamity.
translations.idiomatic: So Yan Cen withdrew toward Wudang under the pretext of mobilizing—actually fleeing Heaven’s warning.

sentences.zh: 今年《比卦》部歲，《坤》主立冬，《坎》主冬至，水性滅火，南方之兵受歲禍也。

translations.literal: This year the Bi hexagram rules the year; Kun governs the Beginning of Winter; Kan governs the Winter Solstice; water’s nature extinguishes fire—the southern army will suffer the year’s bane.
translations.idiomatic: This year’s ruling hexagram is Bi; Kun holds Beginning of Winter, Kan the solstice; water quenches fire—southern armies will bear the year’s curse.

sentences.zh: 德在中宮，刑在木，木勝土，刑制德，今年兵事畢已，中國安寧之效也。

translations.literal: Virtue lies in the central palace; punishment lies in wood; wood overcomes earth; punishment restrains virtue—this year military affairs will end, an omen of peace within the Middle Kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Virtue sits in the central palace, retribution in wood; wood prevails over earth and checks virtue—campaigns will close and the heartland will rest.

sentences.zh: 五七之家三十五姓，彭、秦、延氏不得豫焉。

translations.literal: The thirty-five surnames of the houses of five and seven do not include Peng, Qin, or Yan.
translations.idiomatic: The thirty-five great lineages of the five-and-seven houses omit Peng, Qin, and Yan.

sentences.zh: 如何怪惑，依而恃之？

translations.literal: How can you marvel and waver, relying on and trusting them?
translations.idiomatic: Why linger in wonder and pin hope on them?

sentences.zh: 《葛累》之詩，『求福不回』，其若是乎！

translations.literal: The poem "Ge Lei" says, "Seeking blessing without turning aside"—can it be like this?
translations.idiomatic: As the "Ge Lei" ode says, "Seek blessing without deviating"—surely that is the course.

sentences.zh: 圖讖之占，眾變之驗，皆君所明。

translations.literal: The divinations of charts and prophecies and the verification of the multitude of changes are all what you, sir, understand.
translations.idiomatic: You already grasp chart lore and the shifting signs that confirm it.

sentences.zh: 善惡之分，去就之決，不可不察。

translations.literal: The distinction between good and evil, the decision to leave or join—one cannot fail to examine it.
translations.idiomatic: Discerning good from evil and choosing where to stand admits no negligence.

sentences.zh: 無忽鄙言！

translations.literal: Do not disregard these humble words!
translations.idiomatic: Do not dismiss this blunt counsel.

sentences.zh: 夫周公之善康叔，以不從管、蔡之亂也；

translations.literal: The Duke of Zhou’s kindness to Kangshu lay in not following the rebellion of Guan and Cai.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou favored Kangshu because Kangshu did not join Guan and Cai’s revolt.

sentences.zh: 景帝之悅濟北，以不從吳濞之畔也。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing’s pleasure with the prince of Jibei lay in not following Wu Pi’s rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing favored the king of Jibei for refusing to follow Liu Pi’s rebellion.

sentences.zh: 自更始以來，孤恩背逆，歸義向善，臧否粲然，可不察歟！

translations.literal: Since Gengshi, those who betrayed grace and turned rebel stand against those who returned to righteousness—good and bad are dazzlingly clear; can one fail to inspect this?
translations.idiomatic: From Emperor Gengshi onward, traitors and loyalists stand in sharp contrast—how can you not see which side is which?

sentences.zh: 良醫不能救無命，強梁不能與天爭，故天之所壞，人不得支。

translations.literal: A fine physician cannot save those Heaven has doomed; the violent cannot contend with Heaven—therefore what Heaven ruins, man cannot prop up.
translations.idiomatic: No doctor cures Heaven-doomed patients; brute force cannot rival fate—what Heaven tears down, mortals cannot shore up.

sentences.zh: 宜密與太守劉君共謀降議。

translations.literal: You ought secretly to plot surrender together with Administrator Liu.
translations.idiomatic: You should quietly confer with Administrator Liu on terms of surrender.

sentences.zh: 仲尼棲棲，墨子遑遑，憂人之甚也。

translations.literal: Confucius was anxious and hurried; Mozi was frantic—such was their extreme care for others.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius hurried from court to court; Mozi rushed about—they cared for humanity that intensely.

sentences.zh: 屠羊救楚，非要爵祿；

translations.literal: The butcher who saved Chu did not crave titles and stipends.
translations.idiomatic: The butcher who rescued Chu sought no fiefs or salary.

sentences.zh: 茅焦幹秦，豈求報利？

translations.literal: Mao Jiao remonstrated with Qin—did he hunt repayment?
translations.idiomatic: When Mao Jiao confronted the Qin court, was he angling for profit?

sentences.zh: 盡忠博愛之誠，憤懣不能已耳。

translations.literal: It was the earnestness of utter loyalty and universal love; indignation could not be stopped.
translations.idiomatic: They acted from devotion and universal compassion—wrath at wrong simply could not be contained.

sentences.zh: 又與仲況書諫之，文多不載，於是仲況與龔遂降。

translations.literal: He again wrote Zhongkuang a letter remonstrating with him; the text was long and is not recorded here; thereupon Zhongkuang and Gong then surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: He sent another lengthy letter to Deng Zhongkuang (omitted here); Deng and Liu Gong then capitulated.

sentences.zh: 龔字孟公，長安人，善論議，扶風馬援、班彪並器重之。

translations.literal: Gong’s courtesy name was Mengong; he was a native of Chang’an, skilled in debate; Ma Yuan and Ban Biao of Fufeng both esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong, courtesy Mengong, was from Chang’an and famed as a debater; Ma Yuan and Ban Biao of Fufeng held him in high regard.

sentences.zh: 竟終不伐其功，潛樂道術，作《記誨篇》及文章傳於世。

translations.literal: Jing in the end did not boast of his merit; he immersed himself in the arts of the Way and composed the "Record of Instruction" and essays that circulated in the world.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jing never vaunted his deeds; he pursued Daoist arts and wrote the "Record of Instruction" and other essays that survived him.

sentences.zh: 年七十，卒於家。

translations.literal: At age seventy he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at seventy.

sentences.zh: 楊厚字仲桓，廣漢新都人也。

translations.literal: Yang Hou, courtesy name Zhonghuan, was a native of Xindu in Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Hou, courtesy Zhonghuan, hailed from Xindu in Guanghan.

sentences.zh: 祖父春卿，善圖讖學，為公孫述將。

translations.literal: His grandfather Chunqing was skilled in chart and weft learning and served as a general for Gongsun Shu.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Yang Chunqing mastered prognostic lore and served Gongsun Shu as a general.

sentences.zh: 漢兵平蜀，春卿自殺，臨命戒子統曰：『吾綈帙中有先祖所傳秘記，為漢家用，爾其修之。

translations.literal: When Han troops pacified Shu, Chunqing killed himself; facing death he admonished his son Tong: "In my book-wrap there is a secret record handed down from ancestors, for Han’s use—do you cultivate it."
translations.idiomatic: When Han forces conquered Shu, Chunqing committed suicide; at the last he warned his son Yang Tong: "In my document case lies an ancestral secret scripture meant for Han—honor and preserve it."

sentences.zh: 』統感父遺言，服闋，辭家從犍為周循學習先法，又就同郡鄭伯山受《河洛書》及天文推步之術。

translations.literal: Tong was moved by his father’s dying words; when mourning ended he left home to study with Zhou Xun in Qianwei the methods of the forebears, and also went to Zheng Boshan of his commandery to receive the Hetu–Luoshu documents and the arts of celestial calculation.
translations.idiomatic: Tong heeded his father’s charge; after the mourning period he traveled to Qianwei to learn ancient methods from Zhou Xun and studied the Hetu–Luoshu texts and astronomical reckoning with Zheng Boshan in his home commandery.

sentences.zh: 建初中為彭城令，一州大旱，統推陰陽消伏，縣界蒙澤。

translations.literal: In the Jianchu era he became magistrate of Pengcheng; the entire province suffered great drought; Tong inferred yin and yang’s waxing and waning and the county precinct received timely rain.
translations.idiomatic: During Jianchu he served as magistrate of Pengcheng during a province-wide drought; by analyzing yin–yang cycles he brought rain to his county.

sentences.zh: 太守宗湛使統求為郡求雨，亦即降澍。

translations.literal: Administrator Zong Zhan had Tong seek rain for the commandery, and rain likewise descended.
translations.idiomatic: Prefect Zong Zhan then asked him to pray for the whole commandery, and rain fell there too.

sentences.zh: 自是朝廷災異，多以訪之。

translations.literal: From this time whenever the court had calamities or prodigies it often consulted him.
translations.idiomatic: After that the court routinely sought his reading whenever anomalies arose.

sentences.zh: 統作《家法章句》及《內讖》二卷角說，位至光祿大夫，為國三老。

translations.literal: Tong composed the "House Method with Commentary" and two fascicles of "Inner Prophecies" with supporting explanations; he rose to Grand Counsellor of the Household and became one of the state’s three elders.
translations.idiomatic: Tong wrote the "House Method with Commentary," two volumes of Inner Prophecies with exegetical notes, rose to grand counselor of the household, and was honored among the state’s three elders.

sentences.zh: 年九十卒。

translations.literal: At age ninety he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died at ninety.

sentences.zh: 統生厚。

translations.literal: Tong begat Hou.
translations.idiomatic: Tong’s son was Hou.

sentences.zh: 厚母初與前妻子博不相安，厚年九歲，思令和親，乃托疾不言不食。

translations.literal: Hou’s mother at first did not get along with Bo, her stepson from a previous wife; when Hou was nine sui he wished to reconcile them, so he feigned illness, neither speaking nor eating.
translations.idiomatic: Hou’s mother quarreled with Bo, her husband’s son by an earlier wife; at nine sui Hou tried to heal the breach by pretending to be mute and fasting.

sentences.zh: 母知其旨，懼然改意，恩養加篤。

translations.literal: The mother grasped his intent, became fearful, changed her attitude, and treated him with deeper affection.
translations.idiomatic: When she understood his purpose she was stricken, relented, and grew far kinder.

sentences.zh: 博後至光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Bo later rose to Grand Counsellor of the Household.
translations.idiomatic: Bo later reached the rank of grand counselor of the household.

sentences.zh: 厚少學統業，精力思述。

translations.literal: Hou in youth studied Tong’s craft; he devoted his energy to reflection and composition.
translations.idiomatic: In youth Yang Hou took up his father’s discipline and threw himself into study and writing.

sentences.zh: 初，安帝永初三年，太白入鬥，洛陽大水。

translations.literal: Earlier, in the third year of Yongchu of Emperor An, Great White entered the Dipper and Luoyang suffered great flooding.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, in Yongchu 3 under Emperor An, Venus entered the Northern Dipper as Luoyang was inundated.

sentences.zh: 時統為侍中，厚隨在京師。

translations.literal: At that time Tong was Palace Attendant; Hou accompanied him in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Tong was then a palace attendant, and Hou was with him at court.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以問統，統對『年老耳目不明，子厚曉讀圖書，粗識其意』。

translations.literal: The court asked Tong; Tong replied: "I am old and my eyes and ears are no longer clear; my son Hou understands the charts and documents and broadly knows their meaning."
translations.idiomatic: The court questioned Yang Tong, who answered: "I am old and dim-sighted; my son Hou reads the prognostic texts and grasps their gist."

sentences.zh: 鄧太後使中常侍承制問之，厚對以為『諸王子多在京師，容有非常，宜亟發遣各還本國。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Deng sent a Regular Palace Attendant with edict to question him; Hou replied that he thought "the many royal sons dwelling in the capital might harbor irregular designs; they should be urgently sent back each to his own kingdom."
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Deng dispatched a eunuch with an edict to interview him; Hou answered that the numerous imperial princes gathered in the capital might stir unrest and ought to be hurried home to their fiefs.

sentences.zh: 』太後從之，星尋滅不見。

translations.literal: The empress dowager followed this; the star soon vanished and was not seen.
translations.idiomatic: She accepted his advice, and the aberrant star shortly disappeared.

sentences.zh: 又克水退期日，皆如所言。

translations.literal: Again he hit the date when the waters would recede, all as he had said.
translations.idiomatic: He also predicted when the flood would ebb, and was proved right.

sentences.zh: 除為中郎。

translations.literal: He was appointed Gentleman of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: He received appointment as gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 太後特引見，問以圖讖，厚對不合，免歸。

translations.literal: The empress dowager specially summoned him and questioned him on charts and prophecies; Hou’s answers did not suit her, and he was dismissed and sent home.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager later called him in to discuss prophecy; his replies displeased her, so he was removed and sent home.

sentences.zh: 復習業犍為，不應州郡、三公之命，方正、有道、公正特征，皆不就。

translations.literal: He again pursued his studies in Qianwei and did not answer summons from provinces, commanderies, or the three dukes; nominations as forthright, possessed of the Way, or impartially eminent—he accepted none.
translations.idiomatic: He resumed study in Qianwei, ignored invitations from regional authorities and the three excellencies, and refused every recommendation for office.

sentences.zh: 時大將軍梁冀威權傾朝，遣弟侍中不疑以車馬、珍玩致遺於厚，欲與相見。

translations.literal: At that time Grand General Liang Ji’s might tilted the entire court; he sent his younger brother, Palace Attendant Buyi, with chariots, horses, and precious curios to present to Hou, wishing to meet him.
translations.idiomatic: When Grand General Liang Ji dominated the court, he sent his brother Liang Buyi—palace attendant—with gifts of carriages, horses, and treasures to court Yang Hou’s acquaintance.

sentences.zh: 厚不答，固稱病救退。

translations.literal: Hou did not respond and repeatedly pleaded illness, begging leave to withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Hou ignored the overture and kept insisting he was too ill to attend court, pressing for dismissal.

sentences.zh: 帝許之，賜車馬錢帛歸家。

translations.literal: The emperor permitted it and granted chariots, horses, cash, and silk for him to return home.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved, awarding vehicles, cash, and silk for his retirement.

sentences.zh: 修黃、老，教授門生，上名錄者三千餘人。

translations.literal: He cultivated Huang–Lao and instructed disciples; those who registered their names exceeded three thousand.
translations.idiomatic: He practiced Huang–Lao teachings and taught disciples—more than three thousand enrolled.

sentences.zh: 太尉李固數薦言之。

translations.literal: Grand Commandant Li Gu repeatedly recommended him.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Commandant Li Gu pressed his case more than once.

sentences.zh: 本初元年，梁太後詔備古禮以聘厚，遂辭疾不就。

translations.literal: In the first year of Benchu, Empress Dowager Liang issued an edict to engage Hou with ancient ritual; he declined illness and did not go.
translations.idiomatic: In Benchu 1 Empress Dowager Liang summoned him with full classical ceremony; he pleaded illness and stayed away.

sentences.zh: 建和三年，太後復詔征之，經四年不至。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianhe the empress dowager again ordered him summoned; through four years he did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianhe 3 she summoned him again, yet four years passed without his appearance.

sentences.zh: 年八十二，卒於家。

translations.literal: At age eighty-two he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at eighty-two.

sentences.zh: 策書吊祭。

translations.literal: An edict ordered condolence offerings.
translations.idiomatic: An imperial rescript authorized mourning sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 鄉人謚曰文父。

translations.literal: His fellow villagers gave him the posthumous title Father of Culture.
translations.idiomatic: Villagers honored him posthumously as "Father Wen."

sentences.zh: 門人為立廟，郡文學掾史春秋饗射常祠之。

translations.literal: His disciples raised a temple for him; the commandery’s literary instructorship clerks, at the spring and autumn community archery feasts, regularly made offerings to him there.
translations.idiomatic: His students built him a shrine, and the commandery’s education officials, at the seasonal community archery assemblies, made sacrifice to him as a standing observance.
